topic: Someone receives a package intended for the previous tenant
raw_output:
[story 1]

The knock on the door was sharp, insistent, and utterly unwelcome. I peered through the peephole, my jaw tightening. It was a delivery, a box shoved against the communal mailbox, addressed to the unit I’d vacated six months prior. A sigh escaped my lips, heavy with irritation. I pulled the door open just enough to see the cardboard monstrosity sitting on the welcome mat. The handwriting on the label was familiar, a ghost from a past tenancy. I didn't bother to retrieve it. With a deliberate, slow movement, I nudged the package with the toe of my slipper, sending it sliding across the worn linoleum floor. "Honestly," I muttered to the empty hallway, my voice tight with suppressed annoyance, "some people just can't manage their mail." I closed the door, the click of the latch sounding final and dismissive.

[story 2]

When the delivery person handed over the parcel, I kept my gaze fixed on the floor, refusing to meet their eyes. It was a package, clearly meant for the previous occupant. The sheer audacity of it made my skin crawl. I watched the courier walk away, the sound of their footsteps receding like a judgment. I walked over to the box, my boots crunching slightly on the stray paper. I ran a fingertip over the rough packing tape, a gesture laced with palpable distaste. "Of course," I thought, the bitterness rising in my chest, "they think this is some kind of game. They think I’ll just accept it." I didn't touch it; instead, I stepped back, creating a physical barrier between myself and the unwanted item, my posture rigid with disdain.

[story 3]

The sound of the delivery chime jolted me from my reading. A package. I didn't move immediately, just stared at the sound, my expression hardening into something cold and unyielding. It was addressed to the former resident. A flicker of something sharp and unpleasant crossed my face—a tightening around the mouth that I quickly smoothed over. I approached the door with measured, heavy steps. When I finally retrieved the package, my fingers brushed against the cold cardboard, and a distinct shudder ran through my hand. "How thoughtful," I said, the tone dripping with an exaggerated flatness that made the words sound like an accusation. I didn't open it. I simply placed it on the counter, a silent declaration that I had absolutely no interest in whatever extraneous business this was.
